story: NSF Critical Mineral Accelerator Engine in Alaska

claims.text: On July 14, 2026, NSF announced 12 new Regional Innovation Engines spanning 20 states.
claims.notes: Announcement date July 14, 2026 confirmed on page. Safe to say 12 Engines across 20 states.

claims.text: One of the new Engines is the NSF Critical Mineral Accelerator Engine in Alaska, led by the University of Alaska Fairbanks.
claims.figure: NSF Critical Mineral Accelerator Engine in Alaska
claims.verbatim_quote: NSF Critical Mineral Accelerator Engine in Alaska, led by the University of Alaska Fairbanks
claims.notes: Corroborated by UAF GI page; UAF Geophysical Institute is the lead unit (Munk is a GI professor).

claims.text: Each new Engine initially receives $15 million over its first two years.
claims.verbatim_quote: Each team...will initially receive an award of $15 million over two years
claims.notes: Page says '$15 million over two years'.

claims.text: Each Engine can eventually receive up to $160 million from NSF over ten years, contingent on meeting milestones.
claims.verbatim_quote: Teams that demonstrate progress on well-defined milestones will have the potential to eventually receive up to $160 million each from NSF over the next decade
claims.notes: Milestone-gated / conditional. Must be framed as potential ceiling, not guaranteed money.

claims.text: The Alaska Engine will use artificial intelligence to help locate mineral deposits.
claims.verbatim_quote: It will also use artificial intelligence to help locate mineral deposits and study new biological methods for extracting minerals.
claims.notes: This is the ONLY AI sentence on the page and the deck's core hook. AI is bundled with biological extraction in the same sentence.

claims.text: Partners in the Engine include NANA Regional Corporation and Alaska Silver.
claims.notes: IMPORTANT: page describes NANA as a partner/supporter (Lance Miller helped develop the NSF application; NANA is proud to help support), and Alaska Silver as project partner. The page does NOT call NANA a co-owner or owner. Do NOT use co-owns framing; use partner.

claims.text: The Engine's approach combines advanced technologies for mining, processing and purification with AI to locate deposits and new biological methods to extract minerals.
claims.verbatim_quote: advanced technologies for mining, processing and purification
claims.notes: Mining/processing/purification + AI + new biological methods for extracting minerals ARE supported. The word biomining never appears; use biological methods. Recovery-from-mine-waste and remediation are NOT on the page and are DROPPED.

claims.text: Alaska contains 56 of the 60 minerals the U.S. Geological Survey considers essential to the nation's economy and national security.
claims.verbatim_quote: Alaska contains 56 of the 60 minerals that the U.S. Geological Survey considers essential to the nation's economy and national security.
claims.notes: Exact phrasing is essential to the nation's economy and national security per USGS, not critical. Attribute the count to USGS via UAF.

claims.text: Gov. Mike Dunleavy and the Alaska Legislature provided $500,000 in one-time funding.
claims.verbatim_quote: Gov. Mike Dunleavy and the Alaska Legislature provided $500,000 in one-time funding.
claims.notes: Page says one-time funding; prefer that over matching.

claims.text: Lee Ann Munk, a UAF geosciences professor, is the Engine's chief executive officer, and Christi Bell is its strategy and innovation officer.
claims.verbatim_quote: chief executive officer of Alaska's new National Science Foundation Innovation Engine
claims.notes: Munk is a geosciences professor in the UAF College of Natural Science and Mathematics. Christi Bell titled strategy and innovation officer.

claims.text: NSF's initial $135 million across its nine inaugural Engines has garnered more than $2 billion in matching commitments.
claims.verbatim_quote: an initial investment of $135 million in taxpayers' dollars across nine inaugural NSF Engines has garnered more than $2 billion in matching commitments
claims.notes: First-cohort (nine inaugural Engines) figure, NOT the Alaska Engine. Frame as program-wide leverage, not Alaska-specific.

claims.text: NANA's Lance Miller endorsed the Engine, tying it to innovation, workforce development and responsible resource development.
claims.verbatim_quote: The goal of this NSF Engine, which brings together partners who are dedicated to innovation, workforce development and responsible resource development that creates lasting benefits for Alaskans, is something NANA is proud to help support.
claims.notes: Quote verbatim confirmed. Miller helped develop the NSF application. His title VP Natural Resources is NOT on this page; attribute simply as Lance Miller of NANA.

claims.text: Gov. Dunleavy framed the award as unlocking Alaska's resource potential and reducing America's dependence on China for mineral processing; Robert McCoy is Geophysical Institute Director and Michelle Rizk is Interim University of Alaska President.
claims.verbatim_quote: further unlock Alaska's resource potential and reduce America's dependence on China for mineral processing
claims.notes: Titles Geophysical Institute Director (McCoy) and Interim University of Alaska President (Rizk) confirmed. Dunleavy China-dependence line is a strong on-page frame safe to use.

claims.text: Neither primary source names any specific AI algorithm, model, dataset, software, or deployment site; the AI is described only in general terms.
claims.verbatim_quote: It will also use artificial intelligence to help locate mineral deposits
claims.notes: LOAD-BEARING HONESTY CHECK CONFIRMED. Across both NSF and UAF pages, artificial intelligence appears in exactly one general sentence about helping locate deposits. No named model, method, dataset, vendor, compute, or site. The NSF page does not mention AI for the Alaska Engine at all. Legitimate deck thesis: the AI is announced, not yet specified.

claims.text: The UAF page names lithium, cobalt, nickel, graphite, germanium, gallium and rare earth elements as example critical minerals, used in batteries, smartphones, computer chips, medical equipment, power grids, aircraft and defense technologies.
claims.verbatim_quote: Minerals such as lithium, cobalt, nickel, graphite, germanium, gallium and rare earth elements are used in batteries, smartphones, computer chips, medical equipment, power grids, aircraft and defense technologies.
claims.notes: ONLY these minerals are named. Do not import antimony, zinc, or others. The only named location is the western Alaska exploration site of Alaska Silver. No Ambler, Pebble, or other named districts/mines; do NOT connect this Engine to Ambler/Pebble.

claims.text: The Engine program runs up to ten years, with continued NSF funding contingent on meeting well-defined milestones.
claims.verbatim_quote: Teams that demonstrate progress on well-defined milestones will have the potential to eventually receive up to $160 million each from NSF over the next decade
claims.notes: Reinforces c4. Frame the $160M as conditional and milestone-gated, never as awarded.

claims.text: More than 500 public comments opposed the Stak Energy North Slope data-center lease and fewer than a dozen endorsed it, with the comment window extended to July 17, 2026.
claims.figure: 500+ opposed / fewer than a dozen for / July 17, 2026
claims.verbatim_quote: More than 500 public comments ... Fewer than a dozen comments endorsed the project.
claims.notes: Background context only, not a slide number for the minerals story. A separate, already-shipped topic; use only if referencing the AI-in-Alaska mood, carefully.

claims.text: Sen. Sullivan secured 56 provisions and a historic $2.6 billion for Alaska in the FY2027 NDAA (advanced 18-9 from committee), including a mandate for grid-impact assessments before executing or renewing an Enhanced Use Lease.
claims.verbatim_quote: before executing or renewing any Enhanced Use Lease (EUL) for military infrastructure or energy production
claims.notes: $2.6B is TOTAL Alaska military money, NOT AI money; never present as an AI figure. Partisan press-release primary. Background context only.

ai_specificity_finding: Both primaries announce the Alaska Engine will use artificial intelligence to help locate mineral deposits, and that single general sentence is the ENTIRE technical description of the AI. Neither the NSF page nor the UAF Geophysical Institute page names any algorithm, model, dataset, software platform, vendor, compute infrastructure, or deployment site. The NSF page does not mention AI in connection with the Alaska Engine at all. The AI is bundled in the same sentence with new biological methods for extracting minerals. Honest thesis: the AI has been announced and funded in principle, but not yet specified. No slide may invent specifics.
